coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescence “turn off−on” nanosensor for sensitivity detection acid phosphatase and inhibitor based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Zhengyi
Li, Ning
Na, Weidan
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we developed a label-free and sensitive fluorescence sensor for acid phosphatase (ACP) and its inhibitor parathion-methyl (PM) detection based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH). Upon addition of MnO2 nanosheets, the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH could be efficiently quenched via a fluorescence resonance energy transfer. ACP could easily catalyze the hydrolysis of L-Ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to ascorbic acid (AA), which could reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+ in acidic environment, leading to dramatically increase of the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH. Quantitative detection of ACP in a broad range from 0.1 to 9 mU mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.027 mU mL−1 could be achieved. The feasibility of the proposed sensor in real samples analysis was also studied and satisfactory results were obtained. Furthermore, the fluorescence assay strategy could also be used for the detection of parathion-methyl (PM) as ACP inhibitor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Acid phosphatase
Parathion-methyl
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The design strategy for ACP and PM detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of GQDs@GSH; (B) The size distribution of GQDs@GSH; (C) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs@GSH; The inset of (C) is the images of GQDs@GSH solution taken under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (D) The XPS spectra of GQDs@GSH.

(A) The SEM image of MnO2 nanosheets; (B) UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheets in absence (black line) and presence (red line) of AA; (C) The TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets; (D) TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets in the presence of AA; (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs@GSH, GQDs@GSH/ACP, GQDs@GSH/AAP, GQDs@GSH/PM, GQDs@GSH/MnO2, GQDs@GSH/MnO2/ACP/AAP and GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP/PM system; (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs@GSH with different concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets (0, 2.5, 5, 8, 11, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50 µg mL−1). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets (30 µg mL−1) system; (B) Effect of reaction time (AAP and ACP) on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets (30 µg mL−1)/AAP(100 µmol mL−1)/ACP (9 mU mL−1) system; (C) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH (black squares), GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets (30 µg mL−1) (red dots) and GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets (30 µg mL−1)/AAP (100 µmol mL−1)/ACP (9 mU mL−1) (blue triangles); (D) Effect of AAP concentration on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets (30 µg mL−1) /ACP (5 mU mL−1) system. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP system with different concentrations of ACP (0, 0.1, 0.4, 1, 2, 3, 5, 7 and 9 mU mL−1); (B) Plot of the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP /ACP system against the ACP concentration, where I0 and I stand for the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP system in the absence and presence of ACP.

The fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP system and GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP system with the interfering substances (100 μmol L−1 K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, cholesterol, glucose, threonine, aspartic acid, tryptophan, arginine, phenylalanine, fructose, 2.5 μmol L−1 BSA, HSA, trypsin, pepsin, con A, uricase, glucose oxidase, lysozyme, TYR, invertase and AchE).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets (40 µg mL−1)/AAP (100 μmol L−1)/ACP (9 mU mL−1) system with different concentrations of PM (0, 0.067, 0.155, 0.422, 1.15, 1.94 and 4.57 µg mL−1); (B) Plot of the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP/PM system against the logarithm concentration of PM. I0 and I stand the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP system in the absence and presence of PM, where I0 and I stand for the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP system in the absence and presence of PM.

The fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP/PM system with the interfering substances (100 μmol L−1 K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, cholesterol, glucose, threonine, aspartic acid, tryptophan, arginine, phenylalanine, fructose, 2.5 μmol L−1 BSA, HSA, trypsin, pepsin, con A, uricase, glucose oxidase, lysozyme, TYR, invertase and AchE).
Schematic illustration of the novel nanosensor for ACP and PM detection.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescence “turn off−on” nanosensor for sensitivity detection acid phosphatase and inhibitor based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Respiratory, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Respiratory, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we developed a label-free and sensitive fluorescence sensor for acid phosphatase (ACP) and its inhibitor parathion-methyl (PM) detection based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH). Upon addition of MnO2 nanosheets, the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH could be efficiently quenched via a fluorescence resonance energy transfer. ACP could easily catalyze the hydrolysis of L-Ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to ascorbic acid (AA), which could reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+ in acidic environment, leading to dramatically increase of the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH. Quantitative detection of ACP in a broad range from 0.1 to 9 mU mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.027 mU mL−1 could be achieved. The feasibility of the proposed sensor in real samples analysis was also studied and satisfactory results were obtained. Furthermore, the fluorescence assay strategy could also be used for the detection of parathion-methyl (PM) as ACP inhibitor.

We developed a sensitive GQDs@GSH-based fluorescence sensor for acid phosphatase and its inhibitor parathion-methyl detection.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes as a biocatalyst could efficiently catalyze almost all metabolic processes with precise specificity and in a controlled manner. This made enzymes of considerable interest in the construction of the sensor. Recently, enzyme-based sensors are generally considered to be simple and sensitive techniques for selective detection of target analytes [1].

In a variety of biological enzymes, acid phosphatase (ACP), as a kind of ubiquitous hydrolase, is widely distributed in nature and is mainly distributed in mammalian fluids and tissues [2,3]. ACP could catalyze the hydrolysis of phosphate monoesters and release free phosphate groups in an acidic environment. ACP can catalyst and facilitate important intracellular physiological changes, but its expression is transformed when human possess many diseases [4]. And it has been proved the level of changes in ACP activity is associated with many diseases, such as kidney disease hyperparathyroidism, prostate cancer, hepatitis, multiple myelom and Gaucher's disease [2,5]. Therefore, clinically, the simple and rapid analysis of ACP activity in blood is of great significance. Over the past decade, various strategies have been explored to monitor ACP activity based on enzymatic catalysis, including chromatographic [6], electrochemical assay [7] and immunoassay [8]. Although all the above methods can quantitatively detect ACP, there are some drawbacks, such as complex preparation of test samples, expensive equipment and poor sensitivity. Fluorescent sensing has the advantages of high sensitivity, low cost, simple operation and real-time in imaging as an excellent detection method for ACP. And optical sensing strategies have been highlighted because of its fast, continuous and real-time characteristics.

Currently, there are many fluorescence sensing strategies for ACP activity detection, but the fluorescence indicators are mainly concentrated on fluorescent polymers and organic dyes [9]. Compared to conventional fluorescent polymers or organic dyes, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess unique optical properties including low toxicity, high biocompatibility and better stability [10,11]. With these attractive advantages of GQDs, many applications have been developed for chem/biosensing and bioimaging platforms based on GQDs [12–14]. Combinations of enzymes and quantum dots have shown great promise for health monitoring, environmental applications and agricultural practices because its unique structural, photophysical properties and excellent specificity can be effectively integrated [1]. For example, Ma et al. constructed a MnO2-CD nanocomposite based sensor to detect glucose because the hydrogen peroxide produced by glucose under the action of glucose oxidase could decompose MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+ ions [15]. Ban et al. presented a sensitive method for organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) determination based on the inhibitory effect of OPs on acetylcholinesterase [16]. Akshath et al. designed a specificity and sensitivity quantum dots-gold nanoparticle fluorescence probe detecting the formaldehyde on the basis of förster resonance energy transfer [17]. Li et al. developed a fluorescence assay of alkaline phosphatase in living cells by using boron-doped graphene quantum dots based on the hydrolysis of adenosine triphosphate [18]. These results indicate that enzyme-based quantum dots sensors have become a hotspot of research.

In this paper, we developed a sensitive fluorescence nanosensor for ACP activity and PM detection based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH). Scheme 1 showed the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH could be quenched by MnO2 nanosheets via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). However, the enzyme of ACP could catalyst the hydrolysis of L-Ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to ascorbic acid (AA), which can reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+ ions, causing sufficient recovery of fluorescent signal. Furthermore, the established fluorescent nanosensor can also be used for the determination of ACP inhibitor. In this work, PM was chosen as the inhibitor of ACP, which could efficiently suppress the catalytic capacity of ACP [19]. When parathion-methyl (PM) was introduced, the activity of the ACP was inhibited, preventing the generation of AA and decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets. Thus, the sensing strategy could also be utilized for enzyme inhibitor detection.

Citric acid (C6H8O7· H2O), glutathione and tetramethylammonium (TMA) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. 2-amino-2-hydroxymethyl-1,3-propanediol (Tris), ascorbic acid (AA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), arginine, cholesterol, glucose, tryptophan, threonine, phenylalanine, aspartic acid and fructose were purchased from Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Sodium L-ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) was purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotech Co., Ltd. Acid phosphatase (ACP) was purchased from TCI Development Co., Ltd. CaCl2, MnCl2, NaCl, KCl and MgCl2 · 6H2O were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Institute of Fine Chemicals. Pepsin, trypsin, human serum albumin (HSA) Bovine serum albumin (BSA), uricase, concanavalin A (con A), glucose oxidase, lysozyme, tyrosinase (TYR), invertase and acetyl cholinesterase (AchE) were purchased from Sino-American Biotechnology Co. Ltd. All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade. The resistivity of distilled water used throughout the study was higher than 18 MΩ cm−1. The Tris–HCl buffer solution was used as a medium for the detection process.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiment was carried on a Philips Tecnai F20 TEM. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) experiment was performed on a JF6700 scanning electron microscope. Fluorescence spectra were collected with a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on Varian GBC Cintra 10e UV–visible Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs@GSH was measured on Edinburgh FLS920 by using an integrating sphere. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB MK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. All pH measurements were performed with a PHS-3C pH meter.

GQDs@GSH were synthesized based on a modified synthesis method in the literature [20]. Typically, 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.15 g of glutathione were mixed together and then the mixture was placed in a 5 mL beaker and heated to 260 °C. After about 3 min, the mixture was liquefied, the color of the solution changed from colorless to light yellow, and then turned brown within 10 min. Subsequently, the liquid was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water and dialyzed for 10 h. Finally, the pure GQDs@GSH were obtained. The obtained GQDs@GSH showed stable blue fluorescence with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 31.6%.

MnO2 nanosheets were prepared according to a method reported previously [21]. Typically, 20 mL mixture containing 12 mL of tetramethylammonium (TMA, 1.0 M) and 2 mL of H2O2 (30 wt%) were added in 10 mL of 0.593 g of MnCl2·4H2O aqueous solution within 15 s. The prepared dark brown suspension was vigorously stirred overnight at room temperature. The bulk manganese dioxide was obtained though centrifugation, washed alternatively with deionized water and methanol for 6 times. The MnO2 nanosheets were prepared via ultrasonication of the aqueous solution of the bulk manganese dioxide.

Different concentrations of ACP solutions were mixed with 100 µmol L−1 AAP and reacted at 37 °C for 40 min. Subsequently, 200 µL purified GQDs@GSH, 40 µg mL−1 MnO2 nanosheets and pH 6.2 Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.01 mol L−1) were added in the above solutions. The mixture was diluted with distilled water to 2.0 mL. The fluorescence spectra were collected after shaking thoroughly for 30 s.

Various concentrations of PM solutions were mixed with 9 mU mL−1 of ACP in 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 100 µmol L−1 AAP was introduced into the system for incubating 40 min. Subsequently, 200 µL purified GQDs@GSH, 40 µg mL−1 MnO2 nanosheets and pH 6.2 Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.01 mol L−1) were added in the above solutions. The mixture was diluted with distilled water to 2.0 mL. The fluorescence spectra were collected after shaking thoroughly for 30 s.

For serum sample detection, drug-free human blood samples were collected from healthy volunteer at the Hospital of Changchun China-Japan Union Hospital. All experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines, and the writing of informed consent for all samples was obtained from human subjects. All the blood samples were obtained through venipuncture and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min after standing for 2 h at room temperature. The supernatant was separated and deproteinized by adding acetonitrile (CH3CN) to the samples (CH3CN: serum = 1:1). After vigorous shaking for 2 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The obtained supernatant was filtered twice. The filtrate was adjusted to neutral pH and then diluted with deionized water. Different concentrations of ACP were added to the diluted serum samples to prepare spiked samples. Real sample analysis was carried out using the procedure described above.

In this work, GQDs@GSH was employed as fluorescent probe to detect ACP activity. The optical characteristics of GQDs@GSH were systematically characterized by employing TEM, fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectroscopy, which were showed in Fig. 1. As shown in Fig. 1A-B, GQDs@GSH was well distributed, showing a spherical morphology with an average diameter of 6.8 nm. Fig. 1C showed GQDs@GSH exhibited the maximal emission at 430 nm (black line) and the strong absorbance at 353 nm (red line). Inset in Fig. 1C showed the GQDs@GSH solution was colorless under visible light and exhibited a strong blue under ultraviolet irradiation (365 nm), further indicating the blue fluorescence properties of GQDs@GSH. The chemical and elemental composition of GQDs@GSH surface was further characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1D, four main peaks at 283, 400, 532 and 166 eV (ascribed to C1 s, N1 s, O1 s and S2p respectively) demonstrated the existence of C, N, O and S elements in GQDs@GSH.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of GQDs@GSH was measured to be 31.6%. The high QY may be attributed to the amination reaction between the carboxyl and epoxy groups on the surface of GQDs and the amine group of GSH. Carboxylic and epoxy groups were commonly used as non-radiative electron-hole complex centers, resulting in the non-emission properties of GQDs. When the amination reaction happened, the number of non-radiative electron–hole complex centers was reduced, thereby increasing the effective emission [22,23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design strategy for ACP and PM detection
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrated the strategy of GQDs@GSH-MnO2 sensor for ACP and PM detection. The basic mechanism of the GQDs@GSH-based ACP sensor included the fluorescent quenching of GQDs@GSH by MnO2 nanosheets and the decomposition of the MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+ ions by AA which produced by AAP under the catalysis of ACP. And the PM detection was based on that ACP activity could be suppressed by PM.

As displayed in Fig. 2A, the obtained MnO2 nanosheets were large and well dispersed in water. In addition, MnO2 nanosheets had broad absorption spectra in the range of 300–700 nm, and when AA was introduced, the aborption of MnO2 nanosheets decreased, indicating MnO2 nanosheets could be reduced by AA (Fig. 2B). The TEM images of MnO2 nanosheets in the absence and presence of AA were showed in Fig. 2C-D. Fig. 2C showed the obtained MnO2 nanosheets presented an obvious sheet-like morphology. Apparently, the lamellar structure of MnO2 nanosheets were destroyed after the ACP enzymatic reaction (Fig. 2D). The results directly corroborated that MnO2 nanosheets decomposed which was originated from the ACP catalyzed substrate hydrolysis. To investigate the feasibility of this proposed sensor for ACP detection, GQDs@GSH were mixed with different reactants. As shown in Fig. 2C, there was no significant change in fluorescence of GQDs@GSH after mixing with AAP, ACP and PM respectively. After the addition of MnO2 nanosheets to GQDs@GSH, the fluorescence intensity decreased greatly. But after adding ACP and AAP simultaneously, MnO2 nanosheets could be effectively reduced to produce Mn2+ ions, leading to the distinct recovery of the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH. Subsequently, PM inhibited the of ACP activity, leading to the fluorescent quenching of GQDs@GSH again.

To clearly explain the quenching mechanism caused by MnO2 nanosheets, UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheets and fluorescence lifetimes in the absence and presence of MnO2 nanosheets were studied. MnO2 nanosheets had broad absorption spectra in the range of 300–700 nm, which overlaps with the fluorescence excitation of the GQDs@GSH (Fig. S1A). This was an essential factor for the generation of FRET [24,25]. Furthermore, the FRET between GQDs@GSH and MnO2 nanosheets was further confirmed by fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. S1B). The related data were listed in Table S1. The fluorescence lifetime of GQDs@GSH (6.98 ns) became shorter after the addition of MnO2 nanosheets (6.29 ns), implying that the dynamic quenching process was the dominant factor in the quenching mechanism.

As displayed in Fig. 2D, a gradual decrease of the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH was observed as the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets increased in the range from 0 to 35 μg mL−1. Then, the fluorescence kept unchangeable with further increasing the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. S2). Therefore, the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH can be modulated by the UV–vis absorbance of MnO2 nanosheets via FRET. And 35 μg mL−1 MnO2 nanosheets was chose for the subsequent experiments.

To study the quenched fluorescence of GQDs@GSH by MnO2 nanosheets could be recovered by AA, the effects of AA on the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets system were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets system gradually increased as the concentration of AA increased. And an excellent linear relationship between I/I0 (where I0 and I stand for the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets system in the absence and presence of AA) and the concentration of AA in the range of 0–45 μmol mL−1 was obtained with a regression coefficient of 0.997.

In order to construct a fluorescence sensor with good sensing performance, we further optimized the detection conditions. In this work, the incubation time were investigated firstly. As shown in Fig. 3A, when MnO2 nanosheets were added to GQDs@GSH, the fluorescence intensity decreased immediately and reached a balance within 30 s. The effect of reaction time (AAP and ACP) on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP system was studied in Fig. 3B. It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH increased to the maximum in 40 min and kept unchangeable with further increase in reaction time. So, the reaction time for ACP and AAP was selected as 40 min. The effect of pH was showed in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH and GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets system almost kept stable in the pH range of 5.8–9.0. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP system increased with the increase of pH (5.8−6.2), and then decreased with the increase of pH (6.2–9.0). In addition, the hydrolysis activity of ACP is better under low pH conditions [26]. Therefore, a pH of 6.2 was selected as the working pH for ACP detection. The effect of AAP concentration on the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/ACP system was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH increased gradually with the increase of AAP concentration and remained stable when the concentration of AAP was more than 100 µmol mL−1. Therefore, 100 µmol mL−1 AAP was selected for the following experiments.

The fluorescence spectrum of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP system with different concentrations of ACP was shown in Fig. 4. It could be seen that as the concentration of ACP increased, the fluorescence intensity of the system gradually recovered. There was a good linear relationship between I/I0 and the concentration of ACP in the range from 0.1 to 9 mU mL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. 4B). The linear regression equation was I/I0 = 1.025 + 0.2539 [ACP], mU mL−1, the detection limit (LOD) was 0.027 mU mL−1. The repeatability of this method for ACP detection was evaluated by measuring the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 0.3 mU mL−1ACP in five replicates, and the RSD was 3.51%. The reproducibility for 0.3 mU mL−1ACP detection obtained with the same method using different equipment by different operator was 4.36% (RSD, (n = 5). In addition, the method we proposed was compared with previous ACP detection strategies in linear range and LOD (Table S2). Compared with other assay methods, the method we proposed offered a comparable or superior LOD and linear range.

Selective recognition was an important feature in evaluating the performance of fluorescence sensing platforms, especially for platforms that had potential applications in biosensing. So to investigate the specificity of the nanosensor for the detection of ACP, we studied the influences of various common coexisting substances including various interfering inorganic salts (K+, Ca2+, Na+), metal ions (Mg2+), biological small molecule (cholesterol, glucose, threonine, aspartic acid, tryptophan, arginine, phenylalanine and fructose) and proteins ((bovine serum albumin (BSA)), human serum albumin (HSA), trypsin, pepsin, concanavalin A (con A) and enzymes (lysozyme glucose oxidase, uricase, tyrosinase (TYR), invertase and acetyl cholinesterase (AchE)) to the detection of 7 mU mL−1 ACP. As shown in Fig. 5, after the addition of above substances, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP (black column) system remained nearly constant and only after the addition of ACP, the fluorescence intensity showed obvious recovered (gray column). It can be seen from Fig. 5 (gray column) that even in the presence of interfering substances, the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP system still worked the same for ACP recognition, demonstrating that the proposed ACP sensor could resist interference from common interfering substances. AA produced by ACP had a highly specific response to GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets system and significantly restore the fluorescence. Whereas, GSH could cause modest fluorescence recovery. It could be eliminated by adding N-ethylmaleimide (NEM), a kind of masking agent that could specially react with the thiol group in GSH [27].

To assess the feasibility of the proposed detection method in actual sample analysis, the proposed fluorescence sensor was used for ACP detection in human serum samples. The result obtained with the standard addition method was shown in Table S3. It could be seen that the recoveries of ACP were in the range 96–108% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 3.47. The above results demonstrated that the proposed assay method could be applied for ACP detection in real samples. The colorimetric method, a standard method for ACP determination is used as a contrast method, which is based on the characteristics of ACP can decompose the phenyl phosphate component, and the phenol reagent is used to determine the phenol content at the corresponding wavelength, then the enzyme activity is calculated accordingly, As can be seen in Table S3, a good agreement can be observed between the proposed assay and colorimetric method, demonstrating the good accuracy of our method for ACP determination.

According to previous reports, OPs were a common ACP inhibitor, including malathion, methyl parathion (PM) and paraoxon, which can efficiently suppress the catalytic ability of ACP [18]. In this work, PM was selected as the inhibitor of ACP. When ACP activity was inhibited by PM, its effectiveness in catalyzing the hydrolysis of AAP is diminished. The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets /AAP/ACP system with different concentrations of PM was shown in Fig. 6. It could be seen that as PM concentration increased, the AA-induced MnO2 nanosheets decomposition decreased, resulting in the decrease of the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH. A good linear relationship was obtained between the relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 and the logarithm of the PM concentration (from 0.067 to 4.57 µg mL−1) with a correlation coefficient of 0.993 (Fig. 6B). The linear regression equation was: I/I0 = 0.089 + 0.226 log[PM], ng mL−1, the LOD was 16.9 ng mL−1. The repeatability of this method for PM detection was evaluated by measuring the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 0.18 µg mL−1 PM in five replicates, and the RSD was 4.23%. The reproducibility for 0.18 µg mL−1PM detection obtained with the same method using different equipment by different operator was 4.93% (RSD, (n = 5). In the method we proposed offered a comparable or superior linear range and LOD (Table S4). These results significantly indicated that the established sensor can be used not only for ACP activity detection, but also for PM determination.

Compared with some reported methods based on MnO2 nanosheets [28–30], we used GQDs@GSH in this work, which usually exhibited larger fluorescence quantum yield, while retaining all the advantages of blank GQDs. Meanwhile, the establish method we provided could be used not only for ACP activity detection in human serum but also for PM detection. This made the established sensing platform we provided was applied for the detection of PM in agriculture and environment.

To investigate the specificity of the nanosensor for PM detection, we studied the influences of various common coexisting substances including various interfering inorganic salts (K+, Ca2+, Na+), metal ions (Mg2+), biological small molecule (cholesterol, glucose, threonine, aspartic acid, tryptophan, arginine, phenylalanine and fructose) and proteins ((bovine serum albumin (BSA)), Human serum albumin (HSA), trypsin, pepsin, concanavalin A (con A) and enzymes (uricase, glucose oxidase, lysozyme, tyrosinase (TYR), invertase and acetyl cholinesterase (AchE)) to the detection of 4.57 µg mL−1 PM. As shown in Fig. 5, after the addition of above substances, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP/PM system still work the same for PM recognition, indicating that the proposed PM sensing platform could resist interference from common interference substances. ( Fig. 7)

In order to verify the applicability of proposed PM sensor in actual sample detection, the fluorescence sensor was applied for PM detection in agricultural and environmental samples, such as tap water, river water, rice and tomato. The recoveries were made by the standard addition method, and the results were showed in Table S5. From Table S5 it could be seen that the recoveries of PM in samples were between 90% and 105% and RSD was lower than 5.31%. The result illustrated the GQDs@GSH/MnO2 nanosheets/AAP/ACP/PM based fluorescence sensor have potential applicability for the detection of PM in environmental samples and agricultural products. In addition, the PM was measured by gas chromatography (GC) as a reference method. As can be seen in Table S5, the results obtained by our proposed method were well consistent with that of GC, indicating the accuracy of our method for PM determination was satisfactory.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a novel fluorescence sensing platform for ACP and its inhibitor PM detection based on GQDs@GSH. The basic principle of the GQDs@GSH-based ACP sensor was the fluorescent quenching of GQDs@GSH by MnO2 nanosheets and the decomposition of the MnO2 to Mn2+ cations by AA, which produced by AAP under the catalysis of ACP. Moreover, the assay method was successfully applied in real sample detection with satisfactory results and good reproducibility, indicating that the proposed method had potential application in the real sample detection of ACP. More importantly, the system also provided a prospective platform for sensitive the detection of PM based on the inhibitory effect of PM on ACP.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21575048 and No.21775052), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin Province, China (No. 20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.09.009.
